text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.

text: Everybody, before we get going, I wanted to tell you about a brand new resource that I released.

text: It's called Mastering Git.

text: It is a free Git course that will teach you everything that you need to know on how to use Git from the ground up in less than 120 minutes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's super easy to follow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't need any specific tools installed other than Git itself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'll teach you everything you need to know from the ground up using the command line and the 20% of commands that are going to give you over 80% of the benefits for you and your day to day engineering career.

text: You may not be sure which ones you should use.

text: And I did that.

text: And that's exactly why I need to record this episode.

text: So let me tell you, not even a story, but just a way that we can relate and be on the same page here.

text: How many times have you started working on something or even been working on something a long time?

text: It could have been years, decades.

text: In my case, I've been developing software for over two decades now, which is kind of even crazy to say and think about that I'm at this age and still developing software and enjoying it.

text: which goes to show it's a very, if you enjoy what you do, it can be a very lengthy career, very rewarding, lucrative, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But what I'm trying to say is here, it doesn't matter if you're a beginner or you're someone who has decades of experience, you're still going to have moments where you feel like an idiot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How many times have you tried to write some code or you wrote some code or you did something and something starts happening or doesn't happen and you're not sure why?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You look at the code.

text: You look at the documentation, everything matches, but it doesn't work.

text: And you know deep down that most likely it's probably something elementary.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's something that's very simple to solve.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the voice in your head, or at least the voice in my head, starts kind of scratching from behind the wall to get my attention.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I can hear a slow whisper, which eventually turns to a little bit louder.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And unfortunately, what is that voice telling you?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're an idiot.

text: And that's not anybody else telling you that.

text: That's yourself telling you that.

text: And I'm not saying that you say that to yourself, but I definitely have.

text: And as someone with two decades of experience, I still say that to myself.

text: I don't mean to.

text: I'm not trying to be negative.

text: But there are times when I still feel like an idiot.

text: Now, again, on the same topic of not recording this, I almost didn't call this feeling like an idiot.

text: But upon, again, further reflection and talking to many, many developers over the years and mentoring many people over the years and teaching many people, speaking to people at conferences and podcasts and videos and...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You name it.

text: What is the most common phrase that I hear from people that almost have some level of imposter syndrome or are questioning their abilities?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, that phrase is, I just feel like an idiot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I feel like I don't know what I'm doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I hear this from people who have been coding for two weeks, two months, two years, 10 years, 20 years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, I've been coding for over two decades.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I probably have more experience in various different parts of development, especially Android.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In an Android, I probably have more experience than 95% of developers in the Android ecosystem, simply because I started the day that the first Android phone came out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I had an app in the app store within a week or so.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that said, I still, just very recently, have issues where I might be updating an application, and maybe I was moving something over to Compose, Jetpack Compose, and I couldn't get it to work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And here I am thinking like, wow, I feel like an idiot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now there's a few things to be said about that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The technology, the churn in which the code is changing, the libraries are changing, the interdependencies of various libraries.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a lot of stuff going on here and I have to remind myself of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a lot of dependencies in between various different libraries, especially in Android.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're going to be working with something, sometimes you need to work with a specific version of Kotlin to use that specific Kotlin compose, whatever plugin or compiler.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you're going to be using some library that's for compose, but you want to use something for live data and perhaps testing, then you got to make sure that those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dependencies match.

text: Now, as of recently, a lot of those version numbers will stay the same, which makes life a lot easier.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, there's still instances where you'll find, hey, this main library is version 1.5.

text: And I'm just making the numbers up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that one is 0.8.

text: But 0.8 goes with 1.5.

text: Why?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's just the way that it's done.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: semantic versioning and one team not wanting to stick with the other team or there's just disconnect i don't know but it makes it very difficult and that's what i'm trying to get at here is is that you have to remind yourself that there's gonna be times where you feel like an idiot and it's okay um it's normal uh it's something that every single developer i have ever spoken to has a problem with
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, here's where I noticed that there's one problem that I have run into and have noticed other people run into the same problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Sometimes when you have a certain level of experience, you start getting into intermediate to senior to staff, et cetera, level engineer, you or even years or decades of experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You feel like you should just know something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You feel like I've been doing this for so long, or I have so much experience in this that I should just know how to do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I shouldn't have to ask for help, but it's okay to ask for help.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's what I'm trying to say.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this goes back to having a beginner's mindset.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't matter what I wrote back in the early 2000s with ASP.net or even pre-ASP.net with PHP and everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Sure, some of that knowledge comes forward with just the basics of software design and things you're going to do, but that syntax that I used back then, that doesn't matter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Most of that stuff doesn't even work anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not supported.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So when you think, well, I've had this much experience, I should know what I'm doing, but I don't, I feel like an idiot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You've got to remind yourself that you're learning something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at that point when you get to, you're being productive, but you're not flying through the code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe you're not in a flow state, but you're, you're engaged enough.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're, you're running into roadblocks and it's frustrating and it's hard.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's sometimes very often, actually you want to quit, but don't.

text: And I've had a podcast about this before.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't quit.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But it's that point when you get there, that's where you're learning.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's where you're expanding your mind.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's where you're growing as an individual, as an engineer, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're not an idiot.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You just don't know how it works or you don't know how it works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you have to remind yourself of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that happened to me yesterday, actually.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I was working in the web technology and I ran a database migration and a particular a bunch of files got updated from running a database migration.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm like, I didn't change anything in these other files.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Why is this happening?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I reached out to a friend of mine who was very senior in this technology that I was working with.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And he runs a very popular, you know, tutorial series and citing, I mean, that's his, his living.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I asked him, I said, Hey, do you know why this, this, this, and this is happening?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And he replies back and he goes, yeah, I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: He goes, that's, I've seen that before too.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I don't know why that happens.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I was like, wow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If he doesn't know, like what the heck?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I realized, you know, there's gotta be a reason.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so I stuck with it for about another four or five hours trying to figure out why this was happening because I wanted it to stop.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now it wasn't gonna cause a problem, but I didn't change when you would run these database migrations, it was just supposed to update some data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But then what it was doing was changing a bunch of files, saying that different data types had changed in the database.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm like, this doesn't make sense.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So long story short, I ended up digging way down into the framework source code that I was working with and found out that, oh, when a database migration is run, it checks the database schema automatically.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if there's a difference in the database schema versus what is stored on file in Git, it'll then update that file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's exactly what was happening.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the next phase was like, well, why is the database different?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I didn't change any of the column types or anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it took me a little bit of time to figure out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm like, oh.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I had done a database backup from Heroku, which is an online platform for, you know, web and API and stuff like that, which was a Postgres database.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And because I was having a problem on this one site that I'm working, it's not really released yet, but I'm working on it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the problem would only happen on Heroku.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: with that particular data set i said well you know what i'm gonna pull the database dump down locally i'm gonna restore it locally and i'm gonna see if i can replicate the problem and i did that i was able to replicate the problem i was able to fix it i wrote the migration to solve that problem but when i would run that migration weird things get updated what i ended up finding out
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: is that the database version on Heroku was older than the database version locally.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And due to this, that database type was different.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So overall, I spent about three days on this task.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I was thinking, wow, that's three days.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's crazy.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no way I can do that at work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I mean, you're going to run into those situations at work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You never plan for them to happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They just do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I'll tell you what, the entire time I felt like an idiot because there are people that look up to me for this technology and ask me questions about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I answer these questions and I didn't know why it worked.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I had to dive real deep into the framework to figure it out.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it took me a lot of time and I felt very frustrated in the fact that I had to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's a normal feeling.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: especially if you are using a library that's supposed to save you time, or maybe it's something from, let's say it's Android, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you have a Gradle dependency that's written by the Android team, and there's just a bug.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you get this really weird edge case.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to be frustrating.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to feel like an idiot sometimes, and that's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That doesn't mean that you are.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's really what I'm trying to get at here, is that you just have to realize that...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Regardless of how much experience you have, there's going to be very frustrating times where you're not going to understand what's going on and you need to reach out for help.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that was really what solved it here for me as I realized I needed to ask somebody and I felt embarrassed and I even told the person, I mean, you know, I got 20 years of experience plus 20 years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you have any clue?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just wondering if you had any clue.

text: Thanks for your time.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so that's what I did.

text: And the fact of doing that, again, someone sent me a DM on Twitter and said, hey, I've noticed the same thing happening.

text: You know, young gentleman helped me out.
